coredata.dc:title: In-situ covalent bonding of carbon dots on two-dimensional tungsten disulfide interfaces for effective monitoring and remediation of chlortetracycline residue

Zhang, Peng
Feng, Bo
coredata.dc:description: In view of the increasing threat of chlortetracycline (CTC) in water to human health and the ecological environment, how to detect and remove CTC in wastewater simply and effectively remains an important and challenging task. Herein, a multifunctional platform for the simultaneous detection and degradation of CTC is developed by in-situ synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) in the two-dimensional (2D) WS2 interface by covalent bonding. The as-prepared 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites can not only be used as an intelligent fluorescence probe for CTC detection with good sensitivity (LOD = 7.5 nM) and selectivity but also has good photodegradation ability for CTC (degradation efficiency attained 85.58%). Moreover, the possible mechanisms for the detection and photodegradation of CTC by 2D WS2/CDs were also investigated by theoretical calculations, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves, and electron spin resonance (ESR) analyses. The exceptional photoelectric properties of 2D WS2/CDs are ascribed to the CDs bound onto the 2D WS2 interface by covalent bonding forming heterojunctions, which effectively promote strong interfacial interactions between CDs and 2D WS2, thereby providing more effective charge transfer and enhancing the detection efficiency and photocatalytic activity. In addition, the possible degradation pathway for CTC was speculated via identification of intermediates and frontier electron density calculation. Overall, this study not only proves that the 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites have great potential for the simultaneous detection and degradation of CTC in aquatic environments but also provides a novel idea for the efficient and stable design of multifunctional nanomaterials for environmental pollution monitoring and purification, which is of great significance to both practical applications and basic research.

head.ce:title: In-situ covalent bonding of carbon dots on two-dimensional tungsten disulfide interfaces for effective monitoring and remediation of chlortetracycline residue

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In view of the increasing threat of chlortetracycline (CTC) in water to human health and the ecological environment, how to detect and remove CTC in wastewater simply and effectively remains an important and challenging task. Herein, a multifunctional platform for the simultaneous detection and degradation of CTC is developed by in-situ synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) in the two-dimensional (2D) WS2 interface by covalent bonding. The as-prepared 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites can not only be used as an intelligent fluorescence probe for CTC detection with good sensitivity (LOD = 7.5 nM) and selectivity but also has good photodegradation ability for CTC (degradation efficiency attained 85.58%). Moreover, the possible mechanisms for the detection and photodegradation of CTC by 2D WS2/CDs were also investigated by theoretical calculations, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves, and electron spin resonance (ESR) analyses. The exceptional photoelectric properties of 2D WS2/CDs are ascribed to the CDs bound onto the 2D WS2 interface by covalent bonding forming heterojunctions, which effectively promote strong interfacial interactions between CDs and 2D WS2, thereby providing more effective charge transfer and enhancing the detection efficiency and photocatalytic activity. In addition, the possible degradation pathway for CTC was speculated via identification of intermediates and frontier electron density calculation. Overall, this study not only proves that the 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites have great potential for the simultaneous detection and degradation of CTC in aquatic environments but also provides a novel idea for the efficient and stable design of multifunctional nanomaterials for environmental pollution monitoring and purification, which is of great significance to both practical applications and basic research.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution caused by antibiotic residues remains one of the main environmental problems involving ecological risks and human health damage [1]. As one of the broad-spectrum antibiotics, chlortetracycline (CTC) is the first tetracyclic compound discovered with multiple substituent groups in the molecular framework [2]. CTC has been widely used in human and animal medicine and food because of its effective antibacterial properties, low cost, increasing growth rate, and feed efficiency [3]. However, higher residues can be easily detected in different aquatic environments, such as groundwater (86.6 ng L−1) and surface water (122.3 ng L−1) due to widespread and unreasonable use [4,5]. These high levels of CTC have adverse effects on human health and ecosystems because they can inhibition of protein synthesis, and microbial growth, and activity, as well as trigger bacterial resistance [5,6]. Therefore, monitoring and removing CTC residues in water is of great significance but it is still an important challenge for water pollution control and remediation to a large extent. In recent years, a series of techniques and methods for detecting and removing CTC pollutants have been developed [7–10]. However, most of the technologies and methods currently available can only be used to detect or remove pollutants individually, which greatly hinders their practical applications. Therefore, the development of a novel strategy with simultaneous identification and removal of CTC in water resources is necessary.

With the continuous development of nanotechnology in recent years, many researchers are strived to develop high-efficiency nanomaterials that can be used to detect or degrade CTC [11–15]. Owing to their physicochemical properties, carbon dots (CDs) and tungsten disulfide (WS2) have drawn significant research attention in CTC detection and degradation [16–21]. However, most of the employed CDs and WS2 nanomaterials can only perform detection or degradation processes separately, which is bound to limit their broader applications. To date, several kinds of nanomaterials have been reported to show promising potential in the integration of pollutant detection and degradation. For instance, Gao et al. first built a novel dual-functional platform based on a CuBi2O4@MOFs (CBO@ZIF-8) composite material that enables simultaneous detection and degradation of tetracycline [13]. In addition, Zhao et al. successfully designed and fabricated a GO/g-C3N4/Ag composite material by the self-assembly of GO nanosheets with intercalated g-C3N4 and Ag NPs [14], which has been demonstrated to simultaneously detect and degrade paraoxon-ethyl in aquatic environments. In simultaneous detection and degradation methods, the contaminant level is defined initially, and then the excess amount of contaminant in water is removed. These processes make water treatment more cost-effective and time-saving [22]. Nevertheless, some existing synthesis methods and strategies for multifunctional nanomaterials are commonly involved in simple physical mixing or multistep reactions [13,23,24], which make it difficult to achieve close contact and strong interactions between different semiconductors and limit their wider applications and large-scale preparation. Given this background, the development of some effective synthesis strategies to improve different semiconductor combinations with interactional ways, and further practical application is urgently needed.

Covalent bonds are one of the most common bonds in chemistry [25–27]. A strong interaction is formed by several neighboring atoms sharing electrons with each other, thus forming a relatively stable chemical structure [28,29]. This type of interaction is an example of intramolecular forces and is much stronger than hydrogen bonds, electrostatic interactions, and van der Waals interactions [25,29]. Covalent bonding can be achieved in two ways: sharing of electrons between atoms of the same kind and sharing of electrons between atoms of different kind [26,28]. Recently, covalent bonding has often been used for fabricating composite nanomaterials owing to its close combination from one material to another, efficient chemical transformation and electronic communication at the interface [25,30,31], and versatility endowed by a variety of functional groups [20,32]. For example, Jiang et al. reported that covalently bound to MoS2/CTF composites are synthesized via an in-situ photo deposition method [33]. More importantly, the strong interfacial interactions between MoS2 and CTF via covalent bonding, promote interfacial charge transfer and separation. Therefore, the MoS2/CTF composites display a better hydrogen evolution rate and photocatalytic stability under visible light irradiation than a single material.

In this study, we postulate that CDs riched in the thiol groups could be covalently coupled with the sulfur on the interface of two-dimensional (2D) transition metal dichalcogenide, which can also improve the photoelectric properties. To test this hypothesis, we prepared 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites through a rapid microwave-assisted in-situ covalent bonding. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the as-prepared 2D WS2/CDs can not only be used as a fluorescence probe platform for CTC detection with good sensitivity and selectivity but also display a good photodegradation ability for CTC. Furthermore, the potential mechanisms for detection and photodegradation of CTC by 2D WS2/CDs were also investigated by theoretical calculation, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves, and electron spin resonance (ESR) analyses. Moreover, the degradation pathway for CTC was speculated via both intermediate identification and frontier electron density calculation. Overall, this study not only proves that the 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites have great potential in the simultaneous detection and degradation of CTC in aquatic environments, but also provides a novel idea for the design of an efficient and stable multifunctional platform for the identification and remediation of harmful pollutions.

Bulk WS2, phosphoric acid, cysteine, and antibiotics such as chlortetracycline (CTC), azithromycin (AZM), amoxicillin (AMX), chloramphenicol (CHL), streptomycin sulfate (STR), and cephalexin (LEX) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Inorganic salt, glucose, ascorbic acid (AA), nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH), adenosine triphosphate (ATP), glutathione (GSH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), ovalbumin (OVA), uric acid (UA) and hydroquinone (HQ) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and further purification was not required. All solutions were prepared and diluted with deionized water obtained from a Milli-Q ultrapure water system.

A transmission electron microscopy (TEM) system (TF 20 FEI Ltd., USA) was used to characterize the microstructures, morphology, and energy-dispersive spectra (EDS) for the nanomaterials. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained by using a Bruker Dimension ICON. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by using a 250 Xi ESCA system (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha+). The phonon-assisted vibrational modes were exploited through the Raman spectrometer (Horiba Evolution). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis data were collected on a Rigaku Smart Lab SE X-ray diffractometer. All the fluorescence spectroscopies were measured using an F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). An Edinburgh Instruments FLSP920 was used to obtain the time-resolved fluorescence decay curves. UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-60 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). The oxidation-reduction potentials were obtained from the traditional three-electrode system (platinum electrode is the counter electrode, the glassy carbon electrode is the working electrode and the Ag/AgCl is the reference electrode). Electron spin resonance (ESR) was measured by using a Bruker EMX PLUS system. Photodegradation intermediates of CTC were identified by using an HPLC-MS (LCMS-8045) system (Hitachi, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed by using a thermal analysis instrument (STA449 F5, Germany) with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from room temperature to 800 °C in air.

On the basis of previous studies, an improved synthesis method was used to prepare 2D WS2 suspension [34]. Simply put, 0.2 g of bulk WS2 was added to 20 mL of a 35% ethanol solution, sealed in a sample bottle, and then sonicated in a bath for 90 min. The 2D WS2 supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 15 min. and the resulting supernatant was carefully removed and kept as the finished product.

The 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites were prepared by microwave-assisted heating. First, 1 mg cysteine and 3 mL phosphoric acid were uniformly dispersed in 7 mL preprepared 2D WS2 suspension with continuous ultrasonic for 10 min. And then, the mixture was heated in a domestic microwave oven under medium heat (350 W) for 120 s, and gradually cooled to room temperature. Subsequently, 30 mL ultrapure water was added to dilute the solution. Then, the primary product was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min and then subjected to dialysis for 24 h in a dialysis bag with a cutoff molecular weight of 500–1000 Da. The 2D WS2/CDs products were obtained under vacuum freeze-drying for 48 h. Finally, the obtained 2D WS2/CDs was stored at 4 °C for subsequent applications.

For the detection assay of CTC, 3 μL of 2D WS2/CDs stock solution (1.516 mg mL−1) was added to the CTC solution at a concentration of 0.01–400 μM for a total volume of 500 μL. Then, the fluorescence intensity was recorded using a fluorescence spectrometer with an emission wavelength of 460 nm. The selectivity toward CTC determination was analyzed using the above detection procedure with the same conditions.

The photocatalytic properties of the 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites were evaluated through the photocatalytic degradation of CTC under light irradiation. First, the 2D WS2/CDs (3 mL) was placed into 100 mL water solution of different concentrations of CTC solutions and continuously stirred in the dark for 30 min. The temperature of the reaction system was maintained at 25 °C by a circulating water system between the double-walled quartz cooling jackets. Then, a 350 W xenon lamp was used to simulate the photocatalytic process with sunlight, and the CTC concentrations at different time intervals (0–60 min) were determined by using a spectrophotometer at a maximum absorption wavelength of 357 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and degradation for CTC in real water
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the practical application potential, river water and wastewater were used to simulate the detection and degradation of CTC in real samples. Actual water samples come from a wastewater treatment plant and the Xiang Jiang River (Xiangtan, China). The actual water samples were centrifuged (8000 r min−1, 10 min) and then filtered using 0.22 m membrane filtration to filter out the large particles. The detection and degradation process of CTC in a real water sample was the same as in deionized water. Briefly, CTC solutions of different concentrations were prepared from real water samples, and then using the developed detection procedures for measurement. The parameter settings were identical with those before. The wastewater (including influent and effluent) and the river water were used as the solution for the preparation of 5 μM and 10 μM CTC-obtained wastewater for a total volume of 100 mL. After the photocatalytic degradation process, the residual CTC concentrations were determined by the UV-Vis absorption spectra.

The Gaussian 16 program was performed to obtain the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), frontier electron densities (FEDs), and the total charge for CTC [35]. The detailed data see support information Table S4.

The 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites were prepared by ultrasonic stripping and microwave-assisted heating. Firstly, 2D WS2 was prepared by sonicating bulk WS2 in water bath. Then, the L-cysteine and the 2D WS2 suspension were used as carbon source and solvent respectively to form CDs riched in the sulfur group under microwave-assisted heating. Meanwhile, the sulfur groups exposed on the CDs surface could be covalently coupled with the sulfur on the interface of 2D WS2 in microwave heating process [36,37]. The microstructures and morphology of 2D WS2 and 2D WS2/CDs samples were characterized by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 1 A–C, TEM, HRTEM, and AFM analysis show that the average thickness of 2D WS2 is approximately 1.0–1.5 nm, indicating that monolayer and few-layer nanosheets are formed from bulk WS2. In particular, the interlayer spacing of 2D WS2 nanosheets is measured to be 0.29 nm (Fig. 1B), which can be attributed to the (101) plane [34]. As illustrated in Fig. 1D, the TEM and HRTEM images of 2D WS2/CDs show that CDs with a diameter of approximately 6.56 ± 0.26 nm are uniformly distributed on the 2D WS2 interface. Besides, the interplanar spacings of 0.29 nm and 0.21 nm correspond to the (101) crystal plane of WS2 and (100) crystal plane of the CDs [34,38], respectively (Fig. 1E). Furthermore, the AFM image of 2D WS2/CDs shows that the thickness of 2D WS2 is increased to 8–9 nm after CDs in-situ loading (Fig. 1F), which is mainly owing to the attachment of the CDs onto the surface of the 2D WS2 nanosheets. Subsequently, the EDS energy spectrum exhibits six major energy spectrum peaks for W, S, C, N, P, and O, while the 2D WS2 does not include N, P, and O, thereby further proving the success of the in-situ synthesized of CDs (Fig. S1A–C). Meanwhile, EDS analysis determines the 2D WS2/CDs sample with a C: N: O: P: S: W molar ratio of 17.36: 1: 1.21: 1.40: 18.16: 7.34. The calculated results indicate that the weight ratio of 2D WS2: CDs is 4.51: 1 [39]. Moreover, analysis of TGA curves reveals that the CDs content in the 2D WS2/CDs composites is 21.57 wt% (Fig S1D) [40].

The effect of the in-situ synthesized CDs on the 2D WS2 interface was investigated using Raman spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the Raman spectrum of 2D WS2 shows two major peaks, which are identified as
E 2g 1
located at 352.2 cm−1 and A1g at 421.3 nm−1 [34]. The
E 2g 1
and A1g Raman modes for the 2D WS2/CDs is shifted marginally toward higher wavenumbers of approximately 0.8 and 0.5 cm−1 compared with pristine material, which shows the interaction of the structure of the CDs and the 2D WS2 and bond formation between the two [41,42]. In addition, the XRD pattern demonstrates that the diffraction peaks of as-prepared 2D WS2 are well-matched to the hexagonal phase of WS2 (JCPDS card no. 87–417) [38]. After CDs bound onto the 2D WS2 interface by covalent bonding, a new broad peak at around 28.38 can be observed in 2D WS2/CDs (Fig. 2B), which is consistent with the characteristic peak of pure CDs (Fig. S2A). To further explore the combination mode between CDs and 2D WS2, FT-IR characterization was performed and as shown in Fig. 2C. The 2D WS2/CDs shows almost the same characteristic vibration modes as 2D WS2. Significantly, the new peak at 539 cm−1 can be attributed to the formation of disulfide bond stretching vibrations between the CDs and the 2D WS2 surface [43]. Moreover, the S–H stretch peak at 2549 cm−1, which appears in the spectrum of L-cysteine and CDs, was no longer observed in the 2D WS2/CDs sample (Fig. S2B). The peak of S–H disappeared is commonly attributed to the thiol functional group successfully conjugating to the 2D WS2 surface, which provides indirect evidence that the CDs is covalently coupled with 2D WS2 surface [44,45].

The elemental and chemical states of the as-prepared samples were examined by XPS to further clarify the interaction between 2D WS2 and CDs. The full XPS scan spectrum is shown in Fig. 2D, the 2D WS2/CDs reveals three other typical peaks: P 2p, C 1 s, and N 1 s, located at 133 eV, 284 eV, and 399 eV, respectively, compared with the 2D WS2. As illustrated in Fig. 2E, the W 4f for 2D WS2 and 2D WS2/CDs can be detected as three predominant peaks from the energy spectrum, including W 4f7/2 (32.95 eV), W 4f5/2 (35.36 eV), and W 4f3/2 (38.35 eV), which is similar to the values reported for the WS2 samples [34]. As shown in Fig. 2F, the high-resolution of S 2p spectrum displays S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 peaks at 161.9 and 163.2 eV [38]. It is worth noting that the new peak at 164.5 eV indicates the existence of bridging disulfides S2 2–, which suggests there are disulfide bonds between CDs and 2D WS2 [41]. A similar phenomenon has been observed in thiol-containing molecules (4-fluorobenzyl mercaptan) conjugating with the MoS2 surface [45]. These results are in basic agreement with those of above FT-IR analysis. Meanwhile, the S 2p spectrum of 2D WS2/CDs shows a blueshift compared with 2D WS2, suggesting charge transfer between 2D WS2 and CDs, which is beneficial to achieving close contacts in heterojunctions [46]. Moreover, it should be noted that the chemical states of 2D WS2/CDs are observed in the C 1 s, N 1 s, and P 2p spectra (Fig. S2C–E). All of these results indicate that the CDs are bound onto the 2D WS2 interface by covalent bonding, rather than by van der Waals or electrostatic interaction.

After determining the morphology, chemical state, and combination mode of the 2D WS2/CDs, the optical performances were also further investigated. Compared with the absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. S3A), the 2D WS2/CDs shows two absorption bands at wavelengths of 383 nm and 278 nm (Fig. 3 A). The band at 383 nm is the exciton absorption band of 2D WS2/CDs, which is caused by the direct gap transition at the K point [47]. The absorption band at 278 nm is due to the optical transition from the valence band to the conduction band [48]. To understand the optical performance of 2D WS2/CDs in detail, the fluorescence properties were studied. As shown in Fig. 3B and C, the fluorescence intensity of 2D WS2/CDs first increased and then decreased in which the fluorescence emission displays excitation-independent behavior. Moreover, the excitation wavelength was 390 nm with a maximum emission of 460 nm. Compared with the pure CDs, the fluorescence of the CDs on the surface exhibits a substantial fluorescence blueshift of 30 nm, which might be due to the CDs dispersed onto the 2D WS2 nanosheet surface through disulfide bonds (Fig. S3B–D). As shown in Fig. 3D, there is no obvious variation in the fluorescence intensity of 2D WS2/CDs after a 1 h time scan. These results suggest that the 2D WS2/CDs has good fluorescence stability and can be used as a candidate material with practical application value.

Then, the selectivity of the 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites with antibiotics was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the fluorescence intensity of 2D WS2/CDs decreases significantly after adding CTC, but the fluorescence intensity of 2D WS2/CDs does not obviously decrease after adding other antibiotics. These results indicate that 2D WS2/CDs has good selectivity for CTC detection. As far as we know, there are common coexisting saccharides, metal ions, amino acids, and so on in actual water samples [49]. To solve the problem of whether the coexisting substances of impurities influence the CTC detection, an interference experiment for CTC with coexisting metal ions, saccharides, protein, and amino acids was further carried out. As shown in Fig. 4C and D, there is no significant change in the fluorescence signal, indicating that the coexisting foreign substances have little effect on the determination of CTC.

In order to further probe the potential sensitive capacity of 2D WS2/CDs for CTC, relevant fluorescence quenching experiments were carried out at different CTC concentrations. As illustrated in Fig. 4E and F, the fluorescence intensity of 2D WS2/CDs decreases with the increasing amount of CTC added. Particularly, in the range of 0.01–10 μM, the fluorescence quenching rate (F0-F/F0) indicated a good linear relationship for logarithmic plots of the CTC concentrations. Meanwhile, the corresponding limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 7.5 nM, at a signal-to-ratio of 3. A comparison between this method and the recently reported CTC optical detection method is listed in Table S1. This detection procedure is superior or basically equivalent to many previously reported methods. Furthermore, the potential of application in the environment was studied through the detection of CTC in actual water samples. After adding different concentrations of CTC, real water samples were tested according to the above procedures. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries of CTC in spiked real samples ranged from 97.28 to 104.63%, indicating that this CTC detection procedure is reliable in real water samples.

Apart from evaluating the ability of 2D WS2/CDs in the sensitive detection of CTC, the potential of 2D WS2/CDs for the degradation of CTC in the environment was further identified. Subsequently, the photocatalytic activity of 2D WS2/CDs was also studied by degradation of CTC solution under light irradiation conditions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the 2D WS2/CDs composite materials can rapidly degrade CTC within the first 30 min after light irradiation, and the degradation rate reach 85.58% after 60 min, which is nearly 3.6 times higher than that of WS2 alone (23.75%). In contrast, the physically mixed sample of 2D WS2 and CDs has the degradation rate of 53.27% under the same conditions, which is much lower than that of the 2D WS2/CDs but higher than 2D WS2. It could be mainly due to the CDs themselves have excellent photoelectric and catalytic properties [50]. In addition, the results indicate that the CTC degradation efficiency depends on the amount of 2D WS2/CDs. As shown in Fig. 5B–D, the experiment results show that the pseudo-first-order kinetics can fit the kinetic data well, and the photodegradation rate constant for 2D WS2/CDs is increased from 0.000574 min−1 to 0.0912 min−1 with increasing addition of 2D WS2/CDs from 0% to 3%. Nevertheless, when the 2D WS2/CDs content increases from 3% to 6%, the photodegradation rate constant is decreased from 0.0912 min−1 to 0.00871 min−1. The reason for this phenomenon may be explained by inner filter effects [35], which can compete for the photons required for the semiconductor at a high 2D WS2/CDs content. Furthermore, the effect of different starting concentrations of CTC on the photocatalytic degradation results shows that the best photocatalytic degradation efficiency is obtained for an initial CTC concentration of 10 μM (Fig. S4A). As presented in Fig. S4B, the practical applicability of this nanocomposite material was evaluated using spiked real water samples, the removal efficiency of the 2D WS2/CDs degradation treatment process ranged from 68.78% to 80.67% at spiked levels of 5 μM and 10 μM, which demonstrated that the proposed treatment procedure for CTC is feasible in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the detection and degradation of CTC
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that quenching mechanisms for the fluorescent nanomaterials mainly include dynamic quenching, static quenching, the inner filter effect, Förster resonance energy transfer, and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [51,52]. Compared to other mechanisms, PET involves a process of electron transfer between an electron acceptor and an electron donor [53–56]. A complex that can return to the ground state without the emission of a photon is formed between the electron donor and the electron receptor in this process [51]. Generally, PET consists of two processes, a-PET and d-PET, which depend on the direction of electron transfer [53]. The lifetimes of CDs is reduced, and there is an energy gap between CDs and quencher, which indicates that the quenching mechanism may be relevant to PET [51]. Since the luminescence from 2D WS2/CDs arises from CDs, the PET process between CDs and CTC may extinguish the luminescence from 2D WS2/CDs. In order to test the possibility of this process, the relative energies of the HOMO and LUMO for CTC were calculated by the density functional theory method at the B3LYP/6-311G* level. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the HOMO and the LUMO of CTC were calculated to be −6.12 eV and −2.12 eV, respectively. Furthermore, the energy levels of the HOMO and the LUMO of CDs were calculated to be −6.28 eV and −4.02 eV according to the empirical formula (Fig. S4C) [57]. These results suggest that electron transfer in the excited state of CTC can occur from the LUMO of CTC to the LUMO of CDs, indicating that electron transfer between CTC and CDs is possible [51,53,57]. Moreover, time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were used to further test the charge transfer and exciton recombination process for 2D WS2/CDs in the absence and presence of CTC [11,58]. As shown in Fig. 6B, the average fluorescence lifetime was measured to be 5.19 ns in the absence of CTC, indicating a fast exciton recombination process [58]. Nevertheless, the lifetime of 2D WS2/CDs decreases to 4.58 ns after the addition of CTC. The shortening of the fluorescence lifetime indicate that dynamic quenching occurs, which further confirms the rapid electron transfer process in the 2D WS2/CDs−CTC system [58,59]. Thus, the HOMO of CTC is excited and then transfer to the HOMO of CDs (Fig. 6C), which results in fluorescence quenching of the CDs.

In order to test the roles of reactive species during CTC degradation. Radicals scavenging experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. S4D, the addition of EDTA has a certain inhibitory effect on the degradation of CTC (60%), while IPA and BQ have a significant inhibitory effect on the decomposition of CTC, and the inhibition efficiency up to 75.4% and 80.2%. To further confirm the existence of active species during the photocatalytic degradation process, ESR spectra were obtained with DMPO and TEMPO as the trapping agents. As described in Fig. 6D and E, no appreciable signal due to •OH and •O 2 - radicals in ESR was observed under dark conditions, but the obvious and strong characteristic signal for DMPO-•OH and DMPO-•
O 2 −
are observed under light irradiation. In addition, h+ became weaker due to the use of TEMPO as the trapping agent under light irradiation (Fig. 6F), revealing the creation of h+ and taking part in the oxidative degradation process of CTC [60]. All these results indicate that h+, •
O 2 −
, and •OH can be generated during photodegradation over 2D WS2/CDs and play an important role in the oxidative degradation of CTC [35,60].

To better understand the mechanism of photocatalysis, UV−DRS and valence band XPS (VB-XPS) spectroscopy were used to further characterize the energy band structure of the as-synthesized photocatalyst. The band gaps for the CDs and the 2D WS2 were estimated to be 2.26 eV, and 2.0 eV [61], respectively (Fig. S5A and B). The VB maxima for CDs and 2D WS2 were determined to be located at 1.65 eV and 1.52 eV, respectively (Fig. S5C and D) [34,38,42]. Based on the results from the analyses above, a potential photodegradation mechanism for the 2D WS2/CDs for CTC can be proposed, which is schematically illustrated in Fig. 6G. First, CTC molecules move into proximity to the CDs surface forming a complex by PET. Second, 2D WS2 and CDs can create electron-hole pairs under light irradiation, and the electrons and holes generated by CDs are transferred to the 2D WS2 interface through the heterojunction. Additionally, the CB (−0.48 eV) of 2D WS2 with accumulated electrons is more negative than the standard redox potential of O2/radical •
O 2 −
(-0.33 eV vs. NHE) [62]. Hence, a further reaction can be taken place with O2 on the catalysis surface to form the radical •
O 2 −
. In addition, partially generated •
O 2 −
radical can be producing •OH radical. Meanwhile, the residual photogenerated h+ at the VB of 2D WS2 is reserved for strong oxidizing stability [63]. Finally, CTC can efficiently degrade into smaller intermediates and further degrade to CO2 and H2O under the action of •
O 2 −
, •OH, and h+ generated by the 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites [35,62]. This efficient accelerate electron transfer and inhibits the recombination process of electron-hole pair owing to the CDs can intimately and uniformly be combined with 2D WS2 nanosheets by covalent bonding. Thereby improving the photocatalytic activity. Besides, the introduction of CDs might enhance light utilization and photodegradation efficiencies. This is mainly because CDs themselves have excellent photoelectric properties, rapid charge mobility, and catalytic properties [50].

To clarify the possible photocatalytic degradation pathway of CTC by 2D WS2/CDs under light irradiation, HPLC–MS analysis and theoretical computation based on frontier electron density calculations were employed. As shown in Fig. S6 and Table S3, a total of 13 major intermediates were detected. Moreover, the frontier electron densities (FEDs) and the total charge of the CTC molecule were obtained through frontier electron density calculations to predict the possible attack positions of CTC [64]. As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S4, the positions at C23, C24, O25 C28, and Cl29 show higher FED2 HOMO values, indicating that the photogenerated h+ maybe prefer to oxidize these positions. C9, O22, O25, and Cl29 also show higher FED2 HOMO + FED2 LUMO values, indicating that they might be attacked by •
O 2 −
and •OH radicals. In addition, C13, C16, C18, and N19 are easily attacked by •
O 2 −
radical due to their positive point charges [8,35,64,65].

Based on the above identification of intermediates, possible attack position prediction, possible degradation mechanisms, and reference to other related studies, potential photocatalytic degradation pathways for CTC can be proposed. As presented in Fig. 7 , first, the CTC molecule (m/z = 478) loses one chloride ion to form an intermediate product with an m/z value of 445 [8]. For pathway one, intermediate product P1(m/z = 444) attacks •OH to form hydroxylated product P2 (m/z = 461), and this intermediate is then transformed to compound P3 (m/z = 433) via dealkylation. For pathway two, compound P5 (m/z = 433) and compound P6 (m/z = 416) are formed through the methylation process and can be attracted by h+ [60]. For pathway three, the intermediate product P1 (m/z = 445) is attracted by radicals and decomposes into P1 (m/z = 403) through the nitro-reduction process. The opening ring products formed by double bond cleavage include compounds P4 (m/z = 359), P7 (m/z = 375), P9 (m/z = 337), and P10 (m/z = 297). Meanwhile, P11 (m/z = 268), P12 (m/z = 230) and P13 (m/z = 297) are formed through further oxidation [35,66]. Eventually, these intermediate products are further transferred to other small molecules or oxidized into CO2 and H2O.

In conclusion, a novel multifunctional nanocomposite was successfully prepared by in-situ covalent bonding of CDs at a 2D WS2 interface. The morphologies, microstructures, and optical properties of this nanocomposite were studied systematically. The as-prepared 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites exhibit fluorescence sensing of CTC detection with good selectivity and sensitivity (LOD = 7.5 nM) and shows a good photodegradation ability for CTC (degradation efficiency achieved 85.58%). The excellent photoelectric performance of 2D WS2/CDs is ascribed to the CDs bound onto the 2D WS2 interface by disulfide bonding forming heterojunctions, effectively promoting the strong interfacial interactions between CDs and 2D WS2, thereby providing more effective charge transfer. Moreover, the underlying mechanisms for the detection and photodegradation of CTC by 2D WS2/CDs were also indicated in-depth. The results suggest that the CTC and CDs can form complexes by PET, which is beneficial for the transfer of an electron from CTC to 2D WS2/CDs. In addition, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of 2D WS2/CDs benefits from the strong interfacial interactions between CDs and 2D WS2 by covalent bonding form heterojunctions, which can accelerate the transfer separation of electrons and holes. Besides, the possible degradation pathway for CTC was speculated via identification of intermediates and frontier electron density calculation. Overall, this study not only proved that a 2D WS2/CDs nanocomposites has great potential for the simultaneous detection and photodegradation of CTC in the aquatic environment, but also provides a novel idea for the design of a proficient and stable multifunctional platform for the monitoring and remediation of harmful pollutants, which is of importance for both practical applications and basic research. Meanwhile, we also hope that this study will provide a new perspective for the design and improvement of multifunctional nanomaterials for environmental pollution monitoring and purification using nanomaterials with different dimensions.
